1: wayyōʾmer ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ qûm ʿălēh ḇêt-ʾēl wĕšeḇ-šām wĕʿăśēh-šām mizbēaḥ lāʾēl hannirʾeh ʾêleḵā bĕḇorḥăḵā mippĕnê ʿēśāw ʾāḥîḵā — "Go up to Bethel and settle there, and build an altar to God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother Esau." ʿĂlēh (Qal imperative of ʿālāh, to go up) — ascending to Bethel is an ʿălîyāh (ascent); Bethel is hill country above the Jordan valley. The description of God as hannirʾeh ʾêleḵā bĕḇorḥăḵā (who appeared to you when you were fleeing) recapitulates the Bethel theophany of ch 28 as the constitutive moment of Jacob's covenant relationship. The command comes after the violence of ch 34; the return to the altar is a covenant-renewal following moral contamination.
2: wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-bêtô wĕʾel kol-ʾăšer ʿimmô hāsirû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēḵār ʾăšer bĕtōḵĕḵem wĕhiṭṭahărû wĕhahălîpû śimlōtêḵem — "Put away the foreign gods among you, purify yourselves, and change your garments." The triad — remove foreign gods (ʾĕlōhê hannēḵār, H5236, strange/foreign gods), purify (ṭāhēr, ritual purification), change garments (ḥālaph śimlāh) — is the preparation formula for meeting a holy God. The identical preparation is commanded before the Sinai theophany in Exod 19:10,14 (wash garments, consecrate). Jacob treats the ascent to Bethel as a holy-mountain approach requiring covenant purity. The foreign gods likely include those buried at the oak of Shechem (v4) and perhaps Rachel's household gods stolen from Laban (31:19).
3: wĕnāqûmāh wĕnaʿăleh bêt-ʾēl wĕʾeʿĕśeh-šām mizḇēaḥ lāʾēl hāʿōneh ʾōtî bĕyôm ṣārātî wĕhāyāh ʿimmādî baddereḵ ʾăšer hālaḵtî — "…the God who answered me in the day of my distress, who has been with me on the way I have gone." Yôm ṣārātî (day of my distress/straits) — ṣārāh (H6869, adversity, distress, tightness — from ṣar, narrow/constricted) describes the crisis of the Esau-pursuit. Hāyāh ʿimmādî (who was with me) — the fulfillment of the Bethel covenant promise: "I will be with you wherever you go" (28:15). Jacob's speech to his household is a covenant-history summary — this is how covenant memory works, connecting the present act of worship to the founding theophany.
4: wayyitĕnû ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ ʾēt kol-ʾĕlōhê hannēḵār ʾăšer bĕyāḏām wĕʾet-hannĕzāmîm ʾăšer bĕʾoznêhem wayyiṭmōn ʾōtām yaʿăqōḇ taḥat hāʾēlāh ʾăšer ʿim-šĕḵem — "They gave Jacob all the foreign gods and the rings in their ears, and Jacob buried them under the oak at Shechem." The earrings (nĕzāmîm, H5141, nose/ear rings) are associated with idolatrous worship — Exod 32:2-3: Aaron commanded the Israelites to remove their golden earrings to make the golden calf. The burial under the oak is a ritual interment — removing the gods from cultic accessibility. The oak at Shechem is the same tree (or its successor) under which Abraham stopped when he first entered Canaan (12:6). The disposal of the gods at the covenant-entry site is symbolically rich.
5: wayyisĕʿû wayhî ḥittat ʾĕlōhîm ʿal-hehārîm ʾăšer sĕḇîḇôtêhem wĕlōʾ rādĕpû ʾaḥărê bĕnê yaʿăqōḇ — "They set out, and a terror of God (ḥittat ʾĕlōhîm) fell on all the cities around them, so that no one pursued Jacob's sons." Ḥittāh (H2847, terror, panic, dread — from ḥātat, to be shattered/dismayed) combined with ʾĕlōhîm — a supernatural dread sent by God. This is the ḥittāh of holy-war tradition: YHWH sends panic/dread ahead of Israel (Exod 23:27-28: "I will send my terror ahead of you"; Deut 11:25: "the dread of you"). After the massacre of ch 34 that threatened to provoke Canaanite retaliation (33:30), the divine terror shields Jacob's household on the journey to Bethel. Covenant danger is met with covenant protection.
6: wayyāḇōʾ yaʿăqōḇ lûzāh ʾăšer bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan hîʾ bêt-ʾēl hûʾ wĕḵol-hāʿām ʾăšer-ʿimmô — "Jacob came to Luz in the land of Canaan — that is, Bethel — with all the people who were with him." The narrative provides the toponymic gloss: Lûz is the pre-Israelite name, Bêt-ʾēl the covenant name Jacob gave it in 28:19. The equivalence note occurs in 28:19 (at the naming), 35:6 (at the return), and will recur in Judg 1:23. The secular toponym and the sacred name coexist in the narrator's memory, preserving both layers of place-history.
7: wayyiḇen šām mizḇēaḥ wayyiqrāʾ lammāqôm ʾēl bêt-ʾēl kî šām niglû ʾēlāyw hāʾĕlōhîm bĕḇorḥô mippĕnê ʾāḥîw — "He built an altar and called the place El Bethel, because it was there that God was revealed to him when he fled from his brother." Niglû (Niphal of gālāh, H1540, to uncover, reveal, expose) — the passive of gālāh is used for divine self-revelation in the prophetic tradition (1 Sam 3:21; Isa 22:14; Jer 11:20). The altar-name ʾēl bêt-ʾēl (God of Bethel) is distinct from the place-name Bethel; Jacob names the place by what God did there, not merely by the place itself. The Niphal plural niglû (they were revealed) has puzzled commentators — perhaps indicating that multiple divine appearances occurred at Bethel (28:13; 31:13; 35:9), or reflecting a liturgical formula.
8: waṯṯāmāṯ debōrāh mêniqet ribqāh waṯṯiqqāḇēr miṯṯaḥat lĕḇêt-ʾēl taḥat hāʾallôn wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô ʾallôn bāḵût — "Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died and was buried under the oak below Bethel, and the place was called Allon Bakuth." Allôn bāḵût = "oak of weeping" — allôn (H437, oak, terebinth) + bāḵût (H1068, weeping, from bāḵāh). Deborah traveled from Haran with Rebekah in 24:59 as her nurse (mêniqet, H3243, one who nurses/suckles). Her burial at Bethel — the covenant-renewal site — is noted without explanation. Some commentators suggest her death implicitly marks Rebekah's own death (whose death is never explicitly narrated in Genesis); both the nurse and the mother are absorbed into the Bethel transition. The named tree (allôn) will become a landmark for generations.
9: wayyērāʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ ʿôḏ bĕḇōʾô mippaddan ʾărām wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōtô — "God appeared again (ʿôḏ, once more) to Jacob when he came from Paddan Aram and blessed him." ʿôḏ (H5750, again, still, yet more) marks this as the third major theophany in Jacob's narrative: the first at Bethel when fleeing (28:13), the second at Mahanaim/Peniel when returning (32:1,24), and now this third at Bethel. The sequential theophanies frame Jacob's entire Haran sojourn as a period between two Bethel encounters — the departure theophany and the return theophany. The blessing formula reaffirms that the covenant continues despite ch 34's violence.
10: wayyōʾmer-lô ʾĕlōhîm šimĕḵā yaʿăqōḇ lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ šimĕḵā ʿôḏ yaʿăqōḇ kî ʾim-yiśrāʾēl yihyeh šĕmeḵā wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô yiśrāʾēl — "Your name is Jacob; your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name." The formal name-change uses qārāʾ (to call) three times in this verse — the word's repetition signals the solemnity of the conferral. This is the second divine confirmation of the Peniel name-change (32:28); the repetition at Bethel in the presence of the household gives the name Israel its official covenant status. The formula lōʾ-yiqqārēʾ šimĕḵā ʿôḏ yaʿăqōḇ (you shall no longer be called Jacob) parallels Abraham's name-change formula in 17:5 (no longer called Abram). Yet the narrative continues to use "Jacob" interchangeably with "Israel" — suggesting the name-change is covenantal identity, not erasure of the old.
11: wayyōʾmer-lô ʾĕlōhîm ʾănî ʾēl šadday pĕrēh ûrĕḇēh gôy ûqĕhal gôyim yihyeh mimmeḵā ûmĕlāḵîm mēḥălāṣeyḵā yēṣēʾû — "God said, 'I am El Shaddai — be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come from your body.'" ʾĒl Šadday (H7706) — the divine name used at the Abrahamic covenant's key ratification (17:1) and the Isaacic blessing on Jacob before his departure to Haran (28:3). Its reappearance here signals that the full covenant is being re-ratified at Bethel. Gôy ûqĕhal gôyim — a singular nation (Israel) and a community/assembly (qāhāl, H6951) of nations — the universal scope of the covenant. Mĕlāḵîm (kings) from Jacob's body anticipates the Davidic monarchy; the same promise was given to Abraham (17:6) and to Rebekah (25:23).
12: wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer nātattî lĕʾaḇrāhām wĕlĕyiṣḥāq lĕḵā ʾetennennāh ûlĕzarʿăḵā ʾaḥăreyḵā ʾettēn ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — "The land I gave to Abraham and Isaac I give to you, and to your offspring after you I give the land." The threefold repetition of ʾettēn (I give) — past (to Abraham and Isaac), present (to you), and future (to your offspring) — compresses the entire land-covenant into a single verse. This is the fourth explicit land-covenant in Jacob's narrative (28:13; 32:13; 33:17-20 implicitly; 35:12 explicitly). The covenant extends backward to Abraham and forward to Jacob's offspring — the temporal span of the promise encompasses both the patriarchal period and the conquest/settlement narrative of Joshua.
13: wayyaʿal mēʿālāyw ʾĕlōhîm bimmāqôm ʾăšer-dibbēr ʾittô — "God went up from him at the place where he had talked with him." The verb ʿālāh (to ascend, go up) marks the conclusion of the theophany — the divine presence that descended for the encounter now withdraws. The same verb appears at the conclusion of the 17:22 theophany (God went up from Abraham). The ascent formula is the narrative signal that the divine speech act is complete and the covenant has been ratified. The narrator notes the place (bimmāqôm) — the specific geographical site remains theologically charged even after the divine withdrawal.
14: wayyaṣṣeḇ yaʿăqōḇ maṣṣēḇāh bammāqôm ʾăšer-dibbēr ʾittô maṣṣeḇet ʾāḇen wayyasseḵ ʿāleyhā nesek wayyiṣṣōq ʿāleyhā šāmen — "Jacob set up a stone pillar (maṣṣēḇāh) at the place where God spoke with him, and poured a drink offering (nesek) on it and poured oil (šāmen) on it." Maṣṣēḇāh (H4676, standing stone/stele) — Jacob set up the same type of pillar at Bethel during his departure (28:18), where he also poured oil. The return completes the vow: in 28:18-22 he set up the pillar, poured oil, and vowed; now he fulfills the vow with a second anointing. The addition of nesek (H5262, libation/drink offering) — not present in 28:18 — elevates the ritual to a full cultic act, anticipating the Levitical libation system (Num 15:5-10).
15: wayyiqrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-šēm hammāqôm ʾăšer dibbēr ʾittô šām ʾĕlōhîm bêt-ʾēl — "Jacob called the name of the place where God spoke with him Bethel." The naming is the second official naming of Bethel — the first was in 28:19 after the ladder-theophany. The repetition confirms the name while emphasizing that the name refers specifically to the speaking of God there (ʾăšer dibbēr ʾittô šām ʾĕlōhîm). The place-name is a permanent theological inscription: this is where God speaks.
16: wayyisĕʿû mibbêt-ʾēl wayhî-ʿôḏ kibrat-hāʾāreṣ lāḇôʾ ʾeprātāh waṯṯēled rāḥēl waṯṯĕqaš bĕliddtāh — "They moved on from Bethel. While still some distance (kibrat-hāʾāreṣ) from Ephrath, Rachel began to give birth and had great difficulty." Kibrat-hāʾāreṣ is a distance unit of uncertain measurement — possibly a few miles; the unit appears only here and in 48:7 (Jacob's repetition of this event). The narrative proximity to Bethel — the theophanic-covenant site — for Rachel's death in childbirth is the tragic counterpoint to the covenant renewal. The covenant is ratified; the beloved dies. Waṯṯĕqaš bĕliddtāh — "she had difficulty (qāšāh, H7185, to be hard/difficult) in her labor."
17: wayhî bĕhaqšōtāh bĕliddtāh waṯṯōʾmer lāh hamĕyalledet ʾal-tîrĕʾî kî-gam-zeh lāḵ bēn — "As she was having great difficulty, the midwife said to her, 'Do not despair (ʾal-tîrĕʾî), for you have another son.'" The midwife's consolation formula ʾal-tîrĕʾî (do not fear) — the same divine consolation formula used throughout the patriarchal narratives (15:1; 21:17; 26:24; 46:3). The midwife administers the covenant-comfort formula in a dying woman's ears. The information "another son" fulfills Rachel's own prayer in 30:24: yōsēp YHWH lî bēn ʾaḥēr (may YHWH add another son). The prayer is answered at the cost of the one who prayed it.
18: wayhî bĕṣēʾt napšāh kî mētāh waṯṯiqrāʾ šĕmô ben-ʾônî wĕʾāḇîw qārāʾ-lô binyāmîn — "As her life was departing — for she was dying — she named him Ben-Oni. But his father named him Benjamin." Ben-ʾônî (H1126) — "son of my sorrow/suffering"; ʾôn or ʾōnî (H205/H4036, trouble, sorrow, iniquity) is Rachel's dying experience. Binyāmîn (H1144) — "son of my right hand" (ben-yāmîn); the right hand is the position of power and favor (Gen 48:13-19; Ps 110:1; Matt 26:64). Jacob's renaming transforms the death-name to a life-name — refusing to allow his son to carry the name of grief. This naming contrast (mother's death-name / father's hope-name) is the most emotionally charged naming episode in Genesis; no other birth involves two names by two different namers at a moment of death.
19: waṯṯāmāṯ rāḥēl waṯṯiqqāḇēr bĕḏereḵ ʾeprātāh hîʾ bêt-lāḥem — "Rachel died and was buried on the road to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem)." The narrator identifies ʾeprātāh with Bêt-lāḥem (Bethlehem, H1035, "house of bread/food") — a retrospective identification for the reader. Bethlehem will be the city of Boaz and Ruth (Ruth 1:19-22; 4:11), the birthplace of David (1 Sam 16:1; 17:12), and the birthplace of the Messiah (Mic 5:2; Matt 2:1). Rachel's tomb at Bethlehem becomes the site Jeremiah invokes when he prophesies "Rachel weeping for her children" (Jer 31:15) — and which Matthew cites for Herod's massacre of the Bethlehem infants (Matt 2:18). The location of the tomb at Bethlehem ties maternal grief to the city of messianic origin.
20: wayyaṣṣeḇ yaʿăqōḇ maṣṣēḇāh ʿal-qĕḇurātāh hîʾ maṣṣeḇet qĕḇurat-rāḥēl ʿaḏ-hayyôm — "Jacob set up a pillar over her tomb; it is the pillar of Rachel's tomb to this day." Jacob's second use of a maṣṣēḇāh in this chapter (cf. v14 at Bethel) — but now for grief rather than covenant. The pillar at Bethel marked the place of theophany; the pillar at Rachel's tomb marks the place of death. The narrator's editorial note ʿaḏ-hayyôm (to this day) is an antiquarian marker — the tomb was still a known landmark when the narrator wrote. Both Bethel and Rachel's tomb were cultic-memorial sites in Israel's subsequent history.
21: wayyisaʿ yiśrāʾēl wayyeṭ ʾohălô mēhāllāh lĕmigdal-ʿēḏer — "Israel moved on and pitched his tent beyond Migdal Eder." Migdal-ʿēḏer (H4029, "tower of the flock/herd" — migdāl = tower, ʿēḏer = flock) is a location near Bethlehem associated with pastoral watch. Micah 4:8 addresses "O tower of the flock (migdal-ʿēḏer)" in a messianic prophecy about the reign coming to Zion — the proximity to Bethlehem and to Rachel's tomb gives the place messianic-pastoral resonance. The use of yiśrāʾēl rather than yaʿăqōḇ for the subject marks this movement with the covenant name, even in the midst of grief.
22: wayhî biškon yiśrāʾēl bāʾāreṣ hahîʾ wayyēleḵ rĕʾûḇen wayyiškaḇ ʾet-bilhāh pîlegeš ʾāḇîw wayyišmaʿ yiśrāʾēl — "Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and Israel heard of it." Reuben's act — šāḵaḇ ʾet-pîlegeš ʾāḇîw (lay with his father's concubine) — is a sexual power-claim in the ANE inheritance tradition: taking the father's concubine signals a bid for household authority (cf. Absalom's seizure of David's concubines in 2 Sam 16:21-22 as a political usurpation; 2 Sam 12:8; 1 Kgs 2:22). The act forfeits Reuben's firstborn birthright — confirmed in Gen 49:3-4 ("you went up to your father's bed") and 1 Chron 5:1-2 (his birthright was transferred to the sons of Joseph). Lev 18:8 and 20:11 prohibit this as a violation of the father. Wayyišmaʿ yiśrāʾēl — "Israel heard it" — the same verb (šāmaʿ) as the name Simeon; the father hears and says nothing immediately.
23: bĕnê lēʾāh bĕḵôr yaʿăqōḇ rĕʾûḇen wĕšimʿôn wĕlēwî wîhûḏāh wĕyiśśāḵār wûzĕḇûlûn — "The sons of Leah: Reuben Jacob's firstborn, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun." The twelve-sons enumeration (vv23-26) is the first complete listing of the tribal fathers in a single context. The order follows maternal grouping rather than birth order: Leah's six first, then Rachel's two, then the maidservants' four. Bĕḵôr yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob's firstborn) — Reuben's status is noted even as the narrative has just recorded the act that will cost him this status (v22). The irony is structural: Reuben is named as firstborn immediately after his birthright is forfeited.
24: bĕnê rāḥēl yôsēp ûḇinyāmîn — "The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin." The brevity of Rachel's entry — two names, no elaboration — after her death in vv18-20 is a deliberate narrative economy. The two named are the youngest of Jacob's sons by age; their placement last among the born-in-Canaan/Haran listing (Benjamin was just born in v18) signals their narrative significance: Joseph will dominate chs 37-50, Benjamin will be the pivot of the Joseph-brothers reconciliation.
25: ûḇĕnê bilhāh šipĕḥat rāḥēl dān wĕnaptālî — "The sons of Bilhah, Rachel's servant: Dan and Naphtali." Dan and Naphtali are identified through Bilhah's relationship to Rachel (not to Jacob directly), maintaining the surrogate-mother legal structure established in ch 30. Their placement before Zilpah's sons (Gad and Asher) follows the order of the surrogate births in ch 30 — Bilhah's children came first.
26: ûḇĕnê zilpāh šipĕḥat lēʾāh gāḏ wĕʾāšēr ʾēlleh bĕnê yaʿăqōḇ ʾăšer yullad-lô bĕpaddan ʾărām — "The sons of Zilpah, Leah's servant: Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan Aram." The closing formula ʾăšer yullad-lô bĕpaddan ʾărām is technically imprecise — Benjamin was born in Canaan (v18), not Paddan Aram — which the narrator is aware of (having just recorded it). This may be a summary formula for the entire list (most were born in Paddan Aram) rather than an assertion about each individual, or a scribal tradition that preserves the Paddan Aram origin of the tribal system.
27: wayyāḇōʾ yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-yiṣḥāq ʾāḇîw mamrĕʾāh qiryat hāʾarbaʿ hîʾ ḥeḇrôn ʾăšer-gār-šām ʾaḇrāhām wĕyiṣḥāq — "Jacob came to his father Isaac at Mamre — Kiriath Arba (that is, Hebron) — where Abraham and Isaac had stayed." The identification of Mamre with Qiryat hāʾarbaʿ (city of Arba, H7153, later Hebron) follows the same narrative convention as the Bethel/Luz and Ephrath/Bethlehem identifications earlier in this chapter. Jacob's return to Mamre/Hebron closes the geographic arc that began when he fled to Haran (28:1-5): he returns to his father's house, fulfilling the Bethel vow condition (28:21: "if I return safely to my father's house").
28: wayhî yĕmê yiṣḥāq mĕʾat šānāh wĕšmōnîm šānāh — "Isaac lived 180 years." The patriarchal age formula provides the numerical data for the genealogical record. Isaac's 180 years: he was born when Abraham was 100 (21:5), making him 60 when Jacob and Esau were born (25:26), 100 when Jacob fled to Haran, and approximately 120 when Jacob returned. The 180 years places Isaac's death after Jacob's return but before Joseph's Egyptian career begins — Isaac is alive through ch 35 but not beyond. Abraham lived 175 years (25:7), Isaac 180, Jacob 147 (47:28) — a descending series in the covenant generations, consistent with the post-flood lifespan pattern.
29: wayyigwaʿ yiṣḥāq wayyāmāt wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw zāqēn ûśĕḇaʿ yāmîm wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô ʿēśāw wĕyaʿăqōḇ bānāyw — "Isaac breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people (wayye'aseph el-ammav), old and full of days; and Esau and Jacob buried him." Wayyigwaʿ (Qal of gāwaʿ, H1478, to breathe one's last, to expire) + wayyāmāt (to die) — the double death formula used for Abraham (25:8) and for Jacob (49:33). Wayye'aseph el-ammav (gathered to his people) — the covenant afterlife formula; death is entrance into the community of the covenant dead. The burial by both sons — Esau and Jacob together — is the narrative counterpart to Ishmael and Isaac burying Abraham (25:9). The estranged sons are reconciled at the patriarch's death. Isaac's death at 180, with both sons present, is the covenant patriarch's death formula: full lifespan, gathered to his people, buried by his sons.
